Patch Set 2:

Jinshan - Looking at the osc machinery for handling asynchronous behavior...

In what sense are asynchronous glimpse locks asynchronous that regular glimpse locks are not?  Both generate async ldlm requests where the ptlrpc wait is skipped, but agls are only requested as part of statahead and do not have an LVB associated, and errors are suppressed.

I've spent a while looking through the code, and I can't figure out the essential difference between a regular glimpse lock (which is asynchronous at the LDLM layer, which seems the essential thing) and an asynchronous glimpse lock.  (Not understanding this difference means I'm having trouble understanding which parts of the behavior I need to use for lock ahead locks.)

Patch Set 2:

I believe I've answered my own question.  New version coming, hopefully later today.

Patch Set 2:

Both glimpse and AGL lock are async on the OSC layer. The difference is that there is no process waiting for AGL lock so that the I/O can be destroyed at any time after the glimpse RPC is issued. The end result is that the OSC needs extra code to handle - this is why it has a dedicated lock callback osc_lock_upcall_agl() for AGL lock.

For the prototype of lock ahead implementation, we don't need to implement it as AGL way in the first step. Therefore, it would be simpler to wait for the lock ahead RPCs to complete before returning to user space in ioctl() call. This way will work if we make the caller of ioctl() to be a dedicated pthread of the test program.

(14 comments)

my main question here is whether we want to split this code on 2 parts like this:

part1. at the open time we call a “request only ioctl”, we can modify it so that client-side only expansion happen by the stride borders; i.e. no server-side expansion, no lock ahead by itself, new IO takes new locks for itself but takes them so there were no conflicts in future - simple small straightforward patch.

part2. the rest of the patch, lock ahead, including all the complexity with glimpses, etc

and it would be interesting to measure the perf difference. if it is about 1-2%, we can think if we really want to introduce all this kind of complexity with no cached data under locks, multiple glimpse AST, possible new races, etc…

One comment - If you're using lock ahead and have a lot of lock contention, you are doing things *badly* wrong.  Lock ahead should only be invoked in a controlled situation by an application/library that knows what it's doing.  So I feel that as long as we maintain correctness, it's OK to handle the contention case poorly.

Patch Set 26:

regarding the contention handled "poorly" - depends what do you define as "poorly". No crashes or hangs or even undue long processing on the server should be happening or it becomes an easy DoS avenue.

How tolerable do we want to be to things like stat(2) returning bad file size? How about O_APPEND writes?

Keep in mind that while your carefully crafted application/library does things right, there are always external actors you do not control: impatient user doing ls (or even reading) output files, background backups/HSM stuff, some other system activities that are not immediately apparent now.

as far as the ofd_dlm - I actually feel like in line with my optimization comment, it will need to undergo a bgger change that will kill a bunch of problems by it.
Instead of processing every lock with an AST (which is somewhat stupid on surface, as I now realize), we probably should coalesce those locks as we add them to the list.
Theb the list processin is (mostly) ok.
Coalescing should be on a per-client boundary.
So basically when you have a file with locks like:

 data - Known size - lock1-client1 - lock2-client2 - lock3-client1 - lock4-client2 - lock5-client3 - lock6-client1

This in the end should produce AST list of no more than 3:
lock6, lock5, lock4
(assuming all three have the lock ahead flag whatever it would be).

Another thing you could do is you could probably send all these ASTs in parallel (at least if the number is below a certain threshold) - so there's no increasingly longer waiting the more clients you have.

Patch Set 26:

ls, etc, shouldn't cause any particular problem.  At least, we've tested it and it doesn't seem to.  It performs OK without causing any real slowdown.  Theoretically hammering the server with stat for size on the lockahead file *over and over* might be bad, but just a user doing stat repeatedly hasn't been problematic.

It would be conceivably possible to DOS a server with this, but I can already build a two node app that can DOS a server without lock ahead, driving up lock counts absurdly high (Just less than one write lock per page) by tightly alternating writes from a bunch of processes on the two nodes.  (There's one in the Cray internal test suite that does it occasionally in newer versions of Lustre.  It's made practical by the removal of the inode mutex usage, letting me throw lots of small write requests quickly from the same node.)  I think the answer to that is to improve lock count/LRU management, since any method of manually requesting locks is going to allow clients build up large lock counts.

Talking about stat and O_APPEND writes - Do you see a way here that those could be wrong in a way that was not already possible?  File size as presented to the user is always race dependent when you've got multiple writers taking turns extending the file size (or, really, even with just one writer).

An O_APPEND write I believe takes a lock from current file size to maximum file size.  I don't think it can get current file size wrong in any *new* way with lock ahead.  Again, doing an O_APPEND write to a file that others are extending in a non-O_APPEND manner is going to be racey already.

I'll reply to the rest of your comment shortly (when I've figured it out ;) ).

OK, I remember now.

General and possibly stupid question - I thought on the client side when the glimpse callback comes, the glimpse callback is effectively *to the lock*, not to the client?

And the lock may not have the latest knowledge of the file size (IE, the associated lvb may be out date), since the client may be updating the file size under some earlier lock.  (say, lock1 your scenario).

Having had time to reflect on it, I remember now - the belief that the glimpse callback asked *the lock on the client* and not *the client* more generally is why I didn't pursue something like what you're suggesting.  I vaguely recall this from a conversation with Andreas (which I may be misremembering).

Perhaps if that's right and we do 'ask the lock on the client' instead of 'ask the client', it could be changed to ask the client...  Seems like a more significant change with the possibility of breaking things...

Patch Set 26:

it is true that when you get a glimpse AST, it comes to the lock.
But the lock on the client should not be storing any sizing information, so glimpse callback hops from the lock to the file and returns the size on the file - so you should be fine here.
oscglimpse_Ast calls cl_object_glimpse that seems to be generic enough.

as for OA=APPEND - indeed we lock the whole thing from current EOF towards infinity, so as long as we don't return anything above real EOF, we should be fine.

Patch Set 26:

I agree with Oleg that while the glimpse CB is to the specific *lock* on the client, the size data is actually retrieved from the *inode* that the lock is protecting, so it should get the same size from a callback on any lock held on that resource (excluding the fact that the size itself may be changing).  That means there is no value to sending multiple glimpses to the same client.

Similarly, there is no value sending any glimpses for extents below the actual object size on the OST, since (barring race conditions that will always be present with glimpses) we know the object size is already at least as large as anything the client could return.

Patch Set 50:

Vitaly pointed something out elsewhere:
The OBD_CONNECT flag for NO_EXPANSION_IO is only required for new clients talking to old servers.  Since the only 'old servers' are a special release Seagate did for limited use by Cray, this is a situation we can avoid in the real world.  (Old clients can interop safely with new servers, they just won't use NO_EXPANSION_IO.  They've got a workaround which doesn't matter for purposes of this patch.)

So I've removed that connect flag.

A general thought on the LDLM flags:
If possible, I would *really* like to keep at LDLM_FL_NO_EXPANSION with the same behavior.  NO_EXPANSION_IO could change without causing much trouble (the problem it exists to fix has a client workaround).  But Cray and Seagate have released client & server versions with lockahead in them, and so far, I've been able to keep this patch set 100% compatible with those.

If we change the NO_EXPANSION_IO flag behavior significantly, that will stop being true.

So: if we can't come up with a much clearer set of LDLM flags, I would very much like to keep them as they are.  I understand if that can't be the case, but if that is so, then I would need to switch to a new compat flag for *this* version of lockahead.  It would be *very* nice to not have to do that.

Jinshan, given that the glimpse problem has existed forever, I don't think we should force this patch which has been trying to land for more than two years to fix it.

So there are two O_NONBLOCK related concerns, and I'll just explain them both to be thorough.

1. (This is the big one...) O_NONBLOCK uses LDLM_FL_BLOCK_NOWAIT to get the nonblocking behavior we have for it today.  That behavior is "don't wait behind group locks, but do cancel other locks".  (Note that O_NONBLOCK group lock requests also use this behavior.)  So that means when I extended LDLM_FL_BLOCK_NOWAIT to get the desired behavior for speculative requests (always fail the request when a conflicting lock is found), I broke the expected O_NONBLOCK behavior.  I corrected this by checking for BLOCK_NOWAIT and NO_EXPANSION in the extent compat code.  (I don't think this can be fixed just on the client.)
2. This caused a second, subtler & less important issue.  Since it's possible to set NO_EXPAND on a file descriptor, that generates non-speculative lock requests with NO_EXPAND set (and not BLOCK_NOWAIT).  These requests have real i/o coming and must work as expected.  But what happens if you open that file descriptor with O_NONBLOCK?  You get NO_EXPANSION and BLOCK_NOWAIT again.  This can be worked around pretty easily on the client by just barring it - NO_EXPANSION and O_NONBLOCK is a bizarre combination, and I thought it was OK to just block it outright.  Vitaly felt no, so we added NO_EXPANSION_IO so we can distinguish between O_NONBLOCK + NO_EXPANSION and lockahead (speculative) locks.

Patch Set 98:

what do you suggest, Andreas?

Patch Set 98:

Sorry, I've been quiet.  I've been working on this internally, trying to figure something out.

I've obviously got a vested interest in not breaking what Cray has already released, but I don't feel we should take on a less than optimal solution just to preserve that either.  I think Jinshan is right about adding an FL_SPECULATIVE flag as a better solution than a double meaning for the NO_EXPANSION flag.

I'm trying to work out some stuff on the Cray/Seagate side to see how hard it will be to keep compatibility.  If I can be sure of that, here's my suggestion:
Add a new OBD feature flag, LOCKAHEADV2 (or something), use that for the version in this patch (So, the existing LOCKAHEAD flag will be for the version supported by Cray/Seagate stuff currently.).  Add the FL_SPECULATIVE flag and use it as suggested by Jinshan.  The NO_EXPANSION flag doesn't change, and NO_EXPANSION_IO is removed, but we reserve the bit used by NO_EXPANSION (in the LDLM flags) for a few releases, so that bit is not reused.  (There are a number of empty bits in the lower 32, so this should be fine.  We'll pick an explicit end version with compile time warning/error set up to remove that reservation, maybe after 2.12 is released?)

About the FL_SPECULATIVE flag, the only user of *just* that will probably be lockahead.  I think the asynchronous glimpse locks will need to also keep BLOCK_NOWAIT for backwards compatibility.  (But I'll check.)

For the Cray side (less of an issue for you guys, but very important to me :) ), I'll modify the Cray client to support *both* versions, the old one that uses NO_EXPANSION, BLOCK_NOWAIT, and NO_EXPANSION_IO and the one, which will use NO_EXPANSION and FL_SPECULATIVE.  This should be pretty straightforward.

This plan is subject to my getting things on the Cray/Seagate side figured out.  Still have questions which need answers there.

But I think it does the right thing for master/2.11 and the future, and lets the current stuff keep working too.  (And none of this has any impact of the MPI I/O library layer stuff.)

Thoughts?

Patch Set 98:

Patrick, thanks for the explanation.  It wouldn't be terrible to have this explanation in the commit comment for future reference, rather than largely lost in the discussion here.

I could imagine O_NONBLOCK and NO_EXPANSION having a legitimate use on the same file - someone wanting to do small low(er) latency writes with a thread pool or async IO, that has a good idea of their IO pattern.

That said, I don't think this combination is something that many people will use any time soon, and we are free to define the semantics for it that we want.  For that matter, we are (IMHO) free to define the Lustre behaviour for O_NONBLOCK as well.  Even sending and waiting for the DLM lock RPC might be considered "blocking", so we could consider changing O_NONBLOCK to behave more like lockahead - send the RPC to the server, but don't wait for the reply, and let the client thread get back to whatever it was doing until the client actually has the lock.

The difficulty is that if O_NONBLOCK is exactly the same as lockahead then it won't cause the server to ever call back conflicting locks, and the O_NONBLOCK client may never make forward progress.  A way around that might be to have the BLOCK_NOWAIT locks send a glimpse to the client holding conflicting (if on a different export and no glimpse has recently been sent) to spur them into cancelling the lock if it is idle.  Hence your introduction of NO_EXPAND_IO.

That said, if we are going to add a third flag here, I'd prefer that the semantics be more clearly separated than NO_EXPAND implying "don't block" and NO_EXPAND_IO.  Something like:

BLOCK_NOWAIT -> send AST on conflict (maybe renamed?)
NO_EXPAND    -> don't expand lock (no other semantics)
SPECULATIVE  -> don't conflict with other locks

This makes it possible to send "expanding speculative" lock requests, for example.

Patch Set 98:

Andreas,

We crossed in transit there on our latest updates...

I think my response to (part of) your latest is pretty clear from reading my most recent.

I worry that if we define O_NONBLOCK to mean "don't wait if there's a lock", then that will limit O_NONBLOCK i/o too much to be useful.  For example, if a single process reads and then tries to write a file, that will fail.  That seems like clearly a step too far, even though technically there's blocking in there.

I don't have anything to add to the middle of your message, but I think you do a good job of exploring the issues here.

I think, really, that most of this is resolved by adding the FL_SPECULATIVE flag.  We can sidestep most of these issues, and I think we at Cray can find a good way to make that work with our existing stuff.

I'm not sure about renaming BLOCK_NOWAIT - Its mean is more complex than sending AST on conflict (that happens without it, and it causes you to not wait behind group locks, among other things) and I'm not sure I grok all of it completely.  It could probably use a rename but...  I'll think about it.  Open to suggestions.

I will also consider what to add to the commit message.

Are you OK with adding the other feature flag and temporarily reserving the NO_EXPANSION_IO bit in the LDLM flags?  Those are my main worries about this.

Patch Set 98:

What about stopping to use BLOCK_NOWAIT at all with NO_EXPANSION, and instead use FL_SPECULATIVE to indicate no waiting should be done?  That avoids the conflict resulting from the use of O_NONBLOCK with lockahead, and clearly separates the semantics of the flags?  I'd be fine with continuing to send BLOCK_NOWAIT (in addition to FL_SPECULATIVE) for lockahead for compatibility with existing servers, if that is required, so long as this is for a limited time?

As for O_NONBLOCK and read vs. write, my thought wasn't that the client wouldn't do _anything_ in this case, but rather that it would send a glimpse without waiting for the result (returning -EWOULDBLOCK), and then the client holding the conflicting lock should drop its lock if it is idle (also using Early Lock Cancel to drop its own read lock) so that forward progress could be made.  That said, if we separate lockahead from O_NONBLOCK then we don't really need to change the semantics of BLOCK_NOWAIT at all.
Uploaded patch set 99.

Patch Set 98:

Ah, thought I'd replied...

Yes, that's my plan with regards to FL_SPECULATIVE, and a good suggestion with respect to keep BLOCK_NOWAIT for client support.

I've added a new CONNECT flag to indicate the new version of lockahead with FL_SPECULATIVE, and the server only advertises that.  But the client advertises both.  I did interop testing and that client works well with an older server.

commit.subject: LU-6179 llite: Implement ladvise lockahead
commit.message:
LU-6179 llite: Implement ladvise lockahead

Ladvise lockahead is a new feature allowing userspace to
request extent locks in advance of the IO which will use
them. These locks are not expanded beyond the size
requested by userspace.  They are intended to make it
possible to address lock contention between multiple
clients resulting from lock expansion.  They should allow
optimizing various IO patterns, notably strided writing.
(Further information in LU-6179)

Asynchronous glimpse locks are a speculative version of
glimpse locks, and already implement the required behavior.
Lockahead requests share this behavior.

Additionally, lockahead creates extent locks in advance
of IO, and so breaks the assumption that the holder of the
highest lock knows the current file size.

So we also modify the ofd_intent_policy code to glimpse
PW locks until it finds one it knows to be in use, taking
care to send only one glimpse to each client.

The current patch allows asynchronous non-blocking lock
ahead requests and synchronous blocking requests.  We
cannot do asynchronous blocking requests, because of
deadlocks that occur in having ptlrpcd threads handle
blocking lock requests.

Finally, this patch also adds another advice to disable
lock expansion, setting a per-file descriptor flag.  This
allows user space to control whether or not lock requests
on this file descriptor will undergo lock expansion.

This means if lockahead locks are not created ahead of IO
(due to inherent raciness) or are cancelled by a competing
IO request, the IO requests that should have used the
manually requested locks will not result in expanded locks.
This avoids lock ping-pong, and because the resulting locks
will not extend to the end of the file, future lockahead
requests can be granted.  Effectively, this means that if
lockahead usage for strided IO is interrupted by a
competing request, it can re-assert itself.

lockahead is implented via the ladvise interface from
userspace.  As lockahead results in a DLM lock request
rather than file advice, we do not use the lower levels of
the ladvise implementation.

Note this patch has one oddity:
Cray released an earlier version of lockahead without
FL_SPECULATIVE support.  That version uses
OBD_CONNECT_LOCKAHEAD_OLD, this new one uses
OBD_CONNECT_LOCKAHEAD.

The client code in this patch is interoperable with that
version, so it also advertises OBD_CONNECT_LOCKAHEAD_OLD
support, but the server version is not, so the server
advertises only OBD_CONNECT_LOCKAHEAD support.

Client support for the original lockahead is slated for
removal after the release of 2.12.  This is enforced with
a compile time version test that will remove support.

Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <paf@cray.com>
